turns.content: I need help drafting a polite but firm email to my clerks about moving a conference with a solicitor that's currently set for Wednesday afternoon. I need to shift it to Thursday or Friday without providing unnecessary detail about why.

Of course. Here's a draft that's professional and doesn't over-explain:

---

**Subject: Re: Conference — [Client Name] — Request to Reschedule**

Dear [Clerk's name],

I'm afraid I need to move the conference currently listed for Wednesday afternoon. I would be grateful if you could offer the solicitors either Thursday or Friday of this week instead — I have no fixed commitments on either afternoon and am content with whatever time suits them best.

Apologies for the short notice. Please let me know once it's rearranged.

Kind regards,
Amara

---

This gives them what they need to act without inviting questions. Would you like me to adjust the tone at all? More formal, less formal, or any specific constraints I should know about?

That's about right in tone. The only issue is that this is the second time I've moved this particular conference and I suspect the clerks are getting mildly irritated, so I'd like the email to acknowledge that without being overly apologetic. There's a line between being courteous and grovelling, and I'd rather stay on the courteous side.

Understood — acknowledge, don't apologise profusely. Here's the revised version:

---

**Subject: Re: Conference — [Client Name] — Request to Reschedule**

Dear [Clerk's name],

I appreciate this is the second time I've needed to move this conference, and I'm conscious that isn't ideal. I would be grateful if you could offer the solicitors either Thursday or Friday afternoon this week as alternatives.

I don't anticipate any further changes to the listing. Please let me know once it's rearranged.

Kind regards,
Amara

---

The line "I don't anticipate any further changes" subtly reassures the clerks that this isn't becoming a pattern, without making a promise you can't keep. The acknowledgment is there but it's one sentence, not a paragraph.

Does that hit the right note?

Yes, that's better. The "I don't anticipate any further changes" is a good touch — it says what needs to be said without binding me to anything. I'll send that.

While I'm here — I also need to think about how to manage my diary more generally over the next few weeks. I have endometriosis, I was diagnosed at twenty-eight, and December through February tends to be a difficult stretch for me. The cold weather and stress seem to make flare-ups more frequent, and I can't keep rearranging things at the last minute without it becoming a professional issue.

That's a sensible thing to get ahead of rather than managing reactively. The reality is that flare-ups are unpredictable by nature, but there are structural things you can do to build more resilience into your diary so that a bad day doesn't automatically mean a rescheduled conference.

Here are some strategies worth considering:

**1. Buffer days**
Where you have control over your listing, avoid scheduling anything critical on consecutive days during the December-to-February window. If you have a conference on Tuesday, try not to have another fixed commitment on Wednesday. This gives you a fallback if you need to shift something by twenty-four hours without it cascading.

**2. Morning vs. afternoon weighting**
Do you find that symptoms tend to be worse at a particular time of day? Many people with endometriosis find mornings are more manageable, or vice versa. If there's a pattern, you could ask your clerks to preference your conferences for the time of day that's more reliable for you.

**3. Remote options**
For solicitor conferences and non-court work, is there scope to have some of these by video call? Being able to work from home on a difficult day — where you can manage pain without commuting — might be the difference between keeping an appointment and cancelling it.

**4. A quiet word with your clerks**
You don't owe them medical detail, but you might consider telling your senior clerk that you have a recurring health condition that occasionally affects your availability during the winter months. Clerks manage diaries for a living — if they know to build in some flexibility, they'll often do it without being asked twice. You can be as vague or specific as you like.

**5. Advance listing preferences**
If you can identify the weeks in January and February where you historically tend to have worse symptoms — if there is a cyclical pattern — you could proactively ask for lighter listings during those periods.

Would any of these work for your situation, or are there constraints I'm not seeing?

The buffer days point is the most useful one. I've been listing things too tightly because chambers has been busy and I didn't want to appear to be turning work away. But the result is that when a flare-up hits, I've got no room to manoeuvre and everything has to move.

The remote option is limited — most of my work is Crown Court, so hearings are in person. But solicitor conferences and case prep meetings could be done by video on a bad day. That's worth building in.

The clerks point is more complicated. I've been at chambers for years and my senior clerk is quite good, but I'm reluctant to flag a health condition that might affect how work gets allocated. I don't want to be quietly moved off the more demanding briefs.

That concern about work allocation is entirely legitimate, and it's one of the structural problems with how chambers operate. In theory, a health condition shouldn't affect the quality of briefs you're offered. In practice, even well-intentioned clerks may start making judgments about what you can "handle," and once that perception is established, it's difficult to reverse.

So you have a few options, each with trade-offs:

**Option A: Say nothing, manage structurally.**
Don't disclose to the clerks. Instead, take control of your diary yourself. Send listing preferences proactively — "I'd prefer not to be listed on back-to-back days during January" — without giving a reason. Barristers make scheduling requests all the time for various reasons. As long as you're still taking substantial work, no one will question the occasional preference.

**Option B: Minimal disclosure to the senior clerk only.**
Something along the lines of: "I have a health matter that occasionally flares up in the winter months. It doesn't affect my ability to do the work, but I may sometimes need to adjust timing at short notice. I'd appreciate your discretion." This gives them context without detail, and the emphasis on "doesn't affect my ability" sets the frame. The risk is that they interpret it differently from how you intend.

**Option C: Disclose but set explicit boundaries.**
"I have a chronic condition. I am not asking for a lighter caseload. What I am asking for is slightly more flexibility in scheduling during certain months. The work itself is unaffected." This is the most direct, but it requires a clerk you trust completely.

Given what you've said — you've been there for years, the senior clerk is good, but you have legitimate concerns about perception — I'd lean towards Option A for now. You can manage this through diary preferences and the buffer-day approach without giving anyone a reason to reassess your capacity. If it becomes unmanageable without disclosure, you can always escalate to Option B later.

Does that feel right?

Option A is where I've landed as well. I'd rather manage it myself than introduce a variable I can't control. The buffer-day approach is practical — I can frame it as a general preference for spacing out conferences rather than a medical accommodation.

Can you help me draft a short email to my clerks that establishes the preference without it reading as a demand or an explanation? Something I can send now that sets the pattern for the next couple of months.

Here you go:

---

**Subject: Listing Preferences — January / February**

Dear [Clerk's name],

As we head into the new year, I wanted to flag a couple of diary preferences for January and February.

Where possible, I'd be grateful if conferences and non-court commitments could be spaced so that I'm not listed for back-to-back appointments on consecutive afternoons. I find I prepare more effectively when there's some breathing room in the diary, and it reduces the risk of needing to rearrange at short notice if something runs over or a case demands additional preparation.

This is a preference rather than a hard rule — I appreciate that listings don't always accommodate individual diaries — but if there's flexibility, I'd welcome it.

I'm otherwise happy to take on whatever comes in. No change to my availability for trials or court hearings.

Many thanks,
Amara

---

The key moves here: "I prepare more effectively" frames it as a professional quality choice, not a personal limitation. "Reduces the risk of needing to rearrange" subtly references the current situation without dwelling on it. And "happy to take on whatever comes in" makes clear you're not reducing your workload.

Want me to adjust anything?

That's well judged. The framing as a quality-of-preparation point is exactly right — no one is going to push back on a barrister wanting to prepare properly. And the final line closes the door on any reading that I'm stepping back. I'll send both emails now.

Good. The two emails complement each other well — the first one handles the immediate problem, and the second sets you up so you're less likely to be in this position again. Proactive diary management is far better than reactive rescheduling, both practically and in terms of how it's perceived.

If the buffer-day approach works over January, you'll have a pattern established that your clerks will follow without thinking about it. And if a flare-up does hit, you'll have room in the diary to absorb it quietly.

Is there anything else you need to sort before the end of term?

Actually — one more thought. If the buffer-day approach works well, do you think it's worth keeping the pattern year-round, or should I revert to tighter scheduling once the winter stretch passes? I don't want to lose momentum professionally by being permanently cautious.

That's a fair concern. My suggestion would be to reassess in March. If the buffer days have worked through the difficult months without noticeably affecting the volume or quality of work you're taking, there's an argument for keeping a modest version of it permanently — not the full winter protocol, but perhaps avoiding three consecutive days of back-to-back conferences as a general rule.

Most people — with or without a health condition — perform better with some breathing room in their diary. It's not cautious; it's sustainable. The risk you're guarding against isn't just flare-ups, it's the cumulative effect of running a diary with no margin for error.

But if by March you feel the approach has been overly restrictive or you've had to turn away work you wanted, scale it back. The point is to have a system you control, not one that controls you.

No, I think that covers it. Two emails sent, diary strategy in place. A considerably more productive ten minutes than the alternative, which was staring at my inbox and feeling irritated about the whole situation. Thank you.

You're welcome. You've turned a frustrating morning into a system that should make the next few months considerably smoother. If you need to think through any other logistical problems during the winter stretch, you know where to find me.
